method: grep-verified every call site of each function an increment touches; cross-checked wrapper.py sequencing, the er-freeze, and tests/valoria/test_combat_*.py for hardcoded expectations that silently stop exercising a changed path.

findings.claim: I2a widens heft(w)/percussion_authority(w) to circumstance-aware signatures 'with defaults reproducing today, byte-identical'. The plan states heft(w, grip, range_avail) and 'percussion_authority likewise read the bundle'. But the ONLY way these functions can become grip-aware and byte-identical simultaneously is if EVERY live call site passes grip=0 today. It does not: grip is already NON-ZERO at strike time for closed poles.
findings.evidence: grep-verified: core.strike (core.py:170-171) calls `heft_resp(attacker.w, cfg)` -> `WP.heft(attacker.w)` and `WP.percussion_authority(attacker.w)` passing ONLY the weapon record `attacker.w` (combatant.py:122: `w` property = WEAPONS[self.weapon]). The grip lives on the COMBATANT (`attacker.grip_position`), NOT on `attacker.w`. Meanwhile wrapper.py:66 writes `c.grip_position=S.grip_target(c, closed, cfg)` per-beat BEFORE any strike, and grip_target returns >0 for a closed unwieldy pole (test_combat_stance.py:23 asserts spear grip_target>0 when closed). So at the moment core.strike fires inside a closed exchange, attacker.grip_position is already >0 for poles. TODAY heft/percussion_authority ignore grip entirely (w-only), so this is harmless. The INSTANT I2a threads grip into heft/percussion_authority, core.strike MUST be updated in the SAME commit to read attacker.grip_position and pass it through -- otherwise either (a) core.strike still passes no grip and gets the ideal default (silently NOT circumstance-aware -- the widening is inert on the damage path, contradicting decision 2's 'choked-up thrust deals identical damage' fix), or (b) core.strike is updated to pass grip but systems.adef_cap (systems.py:217) / systems.select_mode (systems.py:281,326) which ALSO call WP.percussion_authority(w) are NOT updated, leaving the sigma/armour-defeat path on ideal-grip percussion while the damage path is on live-grip percussion -- internally inconsistent combat math for the SAME blow.
findings.recommendation: I2a must NOT be sold as behavior-preserving unless it is a pure signature-widening with ideal defaults AND every existing caller (core.strike, adef_cap, select_mode's two WP.percussion_authority calls, puncture_pressure which calls percussion_authority) is verified to pass no grip / grip=0. Make the byte-identity claim concrete: run the full roster through heft/percussion_authority at the OLD signature vs the NEW signature-with-defaults and assert equality, AND grep-assert that no caller passes a non-default grip in I2a. Defer ALL grip-threading of percussion_authority to a single later re-baseline increment that updates core.strike + adef_cap + select_mode ATOMICALLY (they are the 3 consumers), never split across commits.

findings.claim: The plan's 'free cascade: reach_sigma / stophit / close_rate / close_unwieldiness all call reach_base' is FALSE as stated for reach_sigma and stophit, and the real cascade risk is the er-freeze. reach_sigma does NOT call reach_base -- it reads a FROZEN cached reach dict (`er`) computed ONCE at engagement start. stophit_sigma reads neither.
findings.evidence: grep-verified reach_base call sites: wrapper.py:32 (`erA=S.reach_base(A,cfg); erB=S.reach_base(B,cfg)` -- frozen into `er` at line 33), wrapper.py:230 (`slip_inside` -- LIVE per-beat), systems.close_unwieldiness (systems.py:142 -- LIVE), which feeds grip_target/close_tempo. reach_sigma (systems.py:409-417) takes `er` as a parameter and reads `er[defender]-er[aggressor]` -- it NEVER calls reach_base; it reads the frozen dict passed from wrapper.py:141. stophit_sigma (systems.py:608-612) reads only `measure_gap` (a wrapper local) -- it does NOT call reach_base at all. close_rate (systems.py:614) reads `rt`/`displ`/tempo -- also does NOT call reach_base. So of the 4 claimed cascade consumers, only close_unwieldiness actually reads reach_base directly; reach_sigma reads a STALE snapshot and stophit/close_rate read derived wrapper locals. If I3 makes reach_base grip-aware but leaves the er-freeze in place, the CLOSED-exchange reach penalty (reach_sigma via frozen er, wrapper.py:141) and the reopen base_gap (wrapper.py:79) keep using OPEN-grip reach while close_unwieldiness/slip_inside/grip_target use LIVE grip-aware reach -- the exact half-switch: some reach consumers on grip-0 semantics, others on live-grip semantics, for the same beat.
findings.recommendation: I3 must land the reach_base grip-awareness AND the er-refresh (wrapper.py:32-33 recomputed per-beat, and the `er` passed to reach_sigma at :141 + the base_gap at :79 rebuilt from live reach_base) in the SAME commit. Do not treat the cascade as automatic: audit each of the 5 er-reads (wrapper.py:37 longer/shorter label, :38 measure_gap, :79 base_gap, :141 reach_sigma, plus the initial :32) and decide per-site whether it should freeze (the longer/shorter LABEL arguably must stay stable within an engagement to avoid role-thrash) or refresh. A naive 'recompute er everywhere' could flip longer/shorter mid-engagement when a pole chokes up, which the reopen logic (aggressor is shorter, defender is longer) reads by identity -- that is a second-order half-switch inside I3 itself.

findings.claim: reach_base is read by BOTH the frozen-er path and the live slip_inside path in wrapper.py, so between commits there is a window where the engagement-start reach (er) and the per-beat reach (slip_inside/close_unwieldiness) disagree for a pole that has gathered in.
findings.evidence: wrapper.py:230-231: `slip_inside = (S.reach_base(defender,cfg) < S.reach_base(aggressor,cfg) and S.reflex(defender,cfg) >= S.reflex(aggressor,cfg))` -- LIVE reach_base, evaluated in the closed exchange AFTER grip_target has set grip_position>0 for a closing pole (wrapper.py:66). Today reach_base is grip-blind so slip_inside == the frozen-er ordering by construction. Once I3 makes reach_base grip-aware, slip_inside will see the CHOKED (shorter) reach while `er`/reach_sigma still see the FULL reach -- the shorter/longer identity used at wrapper.py:37,79,244,247 (reopen_moment gating: `aggressor is shorter`, `defender is longer`) was derived from the FROZEN full-reach er, so a pole that chokes could simultaneously be 'longer' (by frozen er) and 'shorter' (by live reach_base in slip_inside). Contradictory reach identity within one beat.
findings.recommendation: Decide the reach model explicitly in I3: either (a) reach_base stays the single source and er is a per-beat cache refreshed from it (then slip_inside and reach_sigma agree), or (b) freeze the longer/shorter LABEL at engagement start but let the magnitude refresh. Add a unit assertion that within a single beat, every reach consumer resolves the same grip-aware reach for the same combatant (no consumer reads a stale snapshot while a sibling reads live).

findings.claim: I1 claims `agility` reads at_grip I_g 'byte-identical at g=0 roster-wide'. agility(w) currently takes only the weapon record and reads derive(w)['MoI'] (the full-MoI at the lead-hand axis), NOT at_grip's I_g. at_grip(w, 0)['I_g'] is NOT identical to derive(w)['MoI'] -- at_grip backs out I_cm and re-adds m*d_g^2 at the slid hand position; at g=0, d_g=PoB so I_g = I_cm + m*PoB^2 which reconstructs I0 ONLY up to the `I_cm = max(0.0, I0 - m*PoB**2)` clamp and float round-trip.
findings.evidence: weapon_physics.py:260 `agility` returns `min(1.0, (AGILITY_REF/max(1e-6, derive(w)['MoI']))**AGILITY_EXP)` -- reads derive(w)['MoI'] directly. weapon_physics.py:462-469 at_grip: `I_cm = max(0.0, I0 - m*PoB**2)`; at g=0, u=0, d_g=PoB, returns `I_g = I_cm + m*d_g**2`. Algebraically I_cm + m*PoB^2 == I0 EXCEPT when the max(0.0,...) clamp fires (a weapon whose I0 < m*PoB^2, i.e. a degenerate/half-sword shifted-origin record where CoM inertia would be negative) -- for those, at_grip(w,0)['I_g'] = 0 + m*PoB^2 != I0. Also a floating-point subtract-then-add round-trip is not guaranteed byte-identical. So agility()'s consumers (defense_affinities dodge/parry via weapon_physics.py:278, and mode_sigma's wind cap via defense_affinities) could shift for any clamped weapon. If agility is migrated to at_grip in I1 but the byte-identity is only APPROXIMATE, the mirror-fairness/determinism guards (test_combat_balance_guard.py) may still pass (they are banded 0.40-0.60) while the affinity spread silently drifts -- a change masked as identity.
findings.recommendation: Before claiming I1 byte-identical, run agility(w) OLD vs `at_grip(w,0)['I_g']`-based NEW across the full roster (including the half-sword forms longsword_halfsword/estoc_halfsword, the shifted-origin records most likely to trip the I_cm clamp) and assert exact float equality, not just the banded mirror guard. If any weapon differs, I1 is a re-baseline, not an identity increment -- annotate it as such rather than letting the generous mirror band absorb it.

findings.claim: test_combat_heft.py hardcodes `test_longsword_anchor_near_one` (abs(heft_resp(longsword)-1.0)<1e-6). I2b re-anchors HEFT_REF ('longsword@ideal==1.0'). If HEFT_REF is re-anchored to a NEW quantity (m_eff*omega_cap^2 KE-style per decision 2) the 1e-6 anchor test will either break (caught) or, if HEFT_REF is hand-set to force 1.0 at longsword, PASS while every OTHER weapon's heft silently re-scales -- the anchor test cannot detect that, and the ordering test only checks spear<arming<longsword<greatsword, not magnitudes.
findings.evidence: test_combat_heft.py:42 asserts longsword heft==1.0 within 1e-6; :29 asserts only the ORDERING spear<arming<longsword<greatsword; :37 asserts greatsword>longsword*1.5. None of these pin an absolute magnitude for spear/arming/greatsword or any of the ~30 OTHER roster weapons. core.damage (core.py:153) computes `HEFT_HEAVY*heft_units` where HEFT_HEAVY=3.0 is calibrated to reproduce the old heavy-class magnitude AT the longsword anchor -- so if I2b's KE reformulation keeps longsword==1.0 but changes the spread (e.g. greatsword 1.6->2.4), every non-anchor weapon's DAMAGE shifts with nothing in the test suite pinning it. The plan flags 'the two existing [PHASE-C]-flagged red tests re-evaluated' but does NOT flag that the GREEN heft tests are magnitude-blind for the non-anchor roster.
findings.recommendation: I2b should add a per-weapon heft snapshot regression (all roster weapons, recorded magnitudes) so the KE re-anchor's effect on every weapon is visible and Jordan-gated, not just longsword. Without it, a re-baseline that holds the anchor and the ordering can pass CI while silently re-tiering damage across the roster -- the classic 'test still passes but no longer covers the changed path'.

findings.claim: I4 changes select_mode to add a close-efficacy factor derived from geo and to make approach_displace read sel_head/sel_dmg. select_mode is called at THREE sites with DIFFERENT `closed` values, and its output (sel_head/sel_dmg) is consumed by FOUR downstream readers. If close-efficacy is added to select_mode but a consumer keeps reading the native head, the selected-vs-native head split goes inconsistent.
findings.evidence: grep-verified select_mode call sites: wrapper.py:69 (per-beat, both A and B, `closed` current), wrapper.py:123-124 (re-select after half-sword form-switch, `closed`). select_mode(c, defender_armor, closed, cfg) ALREADY receives `closed` (systems.py:302) but the current body IGNORES it (no `closed` read in the function -- confirmed by reading systems.py:316-335). Downstream consumers of the SELECTED head/dmg: core.strike (core.py:169 `getattr(attacker,'sel_head',None) or attacker.head`), systems.armor_defeat_sigma (systems.py:345 `getattr(aggressor,'sel_head',None)`), systems.adef_cap (systems.py:215 head override), systems.legibility (systems.py:427 `getattr(aggressor,'sel_dmg',None)`). approach_displace (systems.py:444-450) currently reads `longer.w['head']` (the NATIVE head, systems.py:448) NOT sel_head -- and it runs in the APPROACH phase where wrapper.py:69 HAS set sel_head but the plan says decision 5 'approach_displace reads sel_head/sel_dmg (falls back to static head when unset)'. RISK: in the approach, sel_head IS set (wrapper.py:69 runs every beat including not-closed), so approach_displace's fallback 'when unset' NEVER fires in the live loop -- it will ALWAYS read the SELECTED head. That is a behavior change on the approach path the moment I4 lands, NOT a fallback-only change. If I4 wires approach_displace to sel_head but select_mode's new close-efficacy hasn't been validated to reproduce the native head on the approach (closed=False), the approach stop-hit-suppression flips for any weapon whose selected head differs from native at open measure.
findings.recommendation: I4 must verify that at open measure (closed=False) select_mode still returns the NATIVE head for every weapon after the close-efficacy factor is added (the efficacy factor must be ~1.0 at open measure by construction), so approach_displace reading sel_head is genuinely equivalent to reading w['head'] on the approach. Add a unit test: for every roster weapon at closed=False, select_mode(...)[1] == w['head'] (except the poleaxe's documented armour-conditional split). Land select_mode's close-efficacy and approach_displace's sel_head read in the SAME commit, with that equivalence test, so the approach path can't be half-migrated.

findings.claim: I5 adds range_utilization -> c.range_avail and threads it into commit_depth and the approach stop-hit. commit_depth is a SINGLE consumer today but its output `commit` fans out to ~10 downstream reads; and the NEW c.range_avail field must be WRITTEN before commit_depth reads it. If the write (wrapper) and the read (commit_depth) land in different commits, commit_depth reads an absent/default field.
findings.evidence: grep-verified: commit_depth is called once (wrapper.py:128). Its returned `commit` feeds: lunge gate (wrapper.py:130), act_cost (:132), recoverability tempo (:133), attack_sigma (:164 via commit), mode_sigma _deep/_shallow (systems.py:194-197), legibility (systems.py:439), indes_steal (:173), overcommit_exposure (:182), reopen_moment gate (:244 `commit>=4`), read_contest legibility (systems.py:529). `range_avail` does NOT exist yet (grep: no match for range_avail anywhere except the plan). The plan (I1) says 'circumstance-state scaffold (facing/range_avail fields defaulting to ideal => identity)' lands in I1, but decision 4's range_utilization(c, measure_gap, cfg)->c.range_avail WRITE lands in I5. So between I1 and I5, range_avail exists as a defaulted field (1.0) and commit_depth ignores it -- fine. The half-switch risk is INSIDE I5: commit_depth must start reading c.range_avail AND the wrapper must start writing range_utilization -> c.range_avail in the SAME commit. If commit_depth reads range_avail while the wrapper still leaves it at the I1 default 1.0, the 'contracting Beta upper support as room vanishes' lever is inert (silently no-op); if the wrapper writes it but commit_depth doesn't read it, the field is dead. Either split leaves a lever half-wired with nothing failing.
findings.recommendation: I5 lands the range_utilization writer (wrapper, per-beat, before commit_depth at wrapper.py:128) and commit_depth's range_avail read ATOMICALLY. Ablation-gate per the plan's own discipline: assert that with range_avail forced to 1.0 the increment is byte-identical to I4 (the empty=>identity check), and that with real range_utilization the commit distribution actually moves beyond noise -- if it doesn't, the lever is cut. Also confirm the rng-draw ORDER in commit_depth is unchanged (it consumes one betavariate at systems.py:520): range_avail must only reshape the Beta PARAMS, never add/reorder a draw, or seeded determinism (test_combat_balance_guard.py:45) breaks.

findings.claim: I6 adds facing state + a facing_target writer + lateral-void term in closing + profile term in reach_sigma/legibility, 'neutral default reproduces I1-I5 exactly'. reach_sigma and legibility are the consumers; the facing field must default such that the profile term is a strict no-op, and the write must not reorder rng.
findings.evidence: reach_sigma (systems.py:409) and legibility (systems.py:419) are the named profile-term consumers. legibility is called from read_contest (systems.py:529) which is in the seeded rng path (consumes rng.random then conditionally rng.randrange, systems.py:533,536). facing does not exist yet (grep: no facing field, no facing_target). facing_target(c, closed, cfg) is a new per-beat WRITE (like grip_target at wrapper.py:66). RISK is low because the plan explicitly ships facing near-neutral, but the profile term added to reach_sigma/legibility must be `+ K*(facing - NEUTRAL)` with facing defaulting to NEUTRAL so the term is exactly 0 -- if it defaults to 0 while NEUTRAL!=0, the empty=>identity claim silently fails and the mirror band (0.40-0.60) may absorb the drift.
findings.recommendation: Assert the facing default equals the facing NEUTRAL constant so both the closing lateral-void and the reach_sigma/legibility profile terms are algebraically 0 at ship. Add the facing_target write in the same wrapper block as grip_target (wrapper.py:66) so field lifecycle is co-located; confirm it adds no rng draw. Snapshot-test that I6 with neutral facing is byte-identical to I5 output on the determinism seeds, not just within the mirror band.

findings.claim: I7 introduces contact.py (grab/pin), rear_clearance penalty into close_tempo/str_demand, hook_affordance, and wires state_graph/capabilities. rear_clearance(w,g) is grip-aware (reads g); its consumers close_tempo and str_demand must both read the SAME grip source, and the new capabilities gates must match the live engine or test_combat_capabilities.py drifts.
findings.evidence: close_tempo (systems.py:56-66) and str_demand (systems.py:81-82) both already read grip_position via wield_heft/close_unwieldiness. rear_clearance is new (grep: no match). test_combat_capabilities.py asserts capabilities.allowed('gap_thrust'/'halfsword'/'percussive_blow', n) matches the live engine physics for every roster weapon (test lines 24-42) -- if I7 adds grab/disarm/pin CAPABILITIES gates (plan decision 8) but test_combat_capabilities.py's test_table_covers_full_roster (line 45: `set(row)==set(C.CAPABILITIES)`) is not updated in the same commit, the roster-coverage assertion breaks; if it IS updated but the new gate predicates don't match contact.py's actual grab_available logic, the sync guarantee the test exists to enforce is hollow. Lower severity because contact.py is a NEW subsystem (no existing consumer to half-switch against) -- the risk is self-contained to I7's own wiring, not a split across a shared primitive.
findings.recommendation: Land contact.py + capabilities grab/disarm/pin gates + the test_combat_capabilities.py roster/predicate update in one commit. Verify the plan's own acceptance ('no grapple path from open measure') via the capabilities gate AND the wrapper branch together. rear_clearance's grip argument must be sourced from c.grip_position at both close_tempo and str_demand call sites (not one live, one default).

findings.claim: The engine's LIVE test job SKIPS the combat tests when numpy is absent (pytest.importorskip('numpy') at the top of every test_combat_*.py). CLAUDE.md notes the lightweight validator job runs WITHOUT numpy. So the per-increment 'pytest tests/valoria' gate may GREEN while every combat identity/ordering assertion was skipped, not run.
findings.evidence: test_combat_heft.py:16, test_combat_recoverability.py:15, test_combat_stance.py:13, test_combat_capabilities.py:14 all call `pytest.importorskip('numpy')`. test_combat_invariants.py:28 and test_combat_balance_guard have importorskip inside the fixture. If an increment is validated in the numpy-less validator environment, all of these are SKIPPED (not failed) -- a byte-identity regression would not trip. This is a half-switch DETECTION gap: the very tests meant to catch an incomplete migration are conditionally inert.
findings.recommendation: Every increment's verification step must run pytest in an environment WITH numpy + the sim modules present, and assert a non-zero collected/passed count for the combat suite (guard against silent all-skip). Treat a skipped combat suite as a RED verification, not a pass.
